label: Discovery Call - Meridian Health Tech
transcript:
AE (Jordan): Thanks for making time today, Sarah. I know you're heads-down on the Q3 planning cycle.

Sarah (VP Operations, Meridian): No worries, we've been meaning to have this conversation for a while. Honestly, the pain is pretty acute right now.

AE: Tell me about that. What's driving the urgency?

Sarah: So we have about 600 people across three offices. Operations runs on Asana, engineering lives in Confluence and Jira, the GTM team is in Google Docs and Slides, and then we have this patchwork of Notion workspaces that individual teams set up on their own. Nobody knows where anything is. I spent 45 minutes last week just trying to find the Q2 OKR review. It's in four different places.

AE: That's a really common pattern at your stage. What does the decision-making process look like if you were going to consolidate some of those tools?

Sarah: I'd be the main driver. We have a budget cycle reset in October, so timing is actually good if we can move fast enough. My CFO would need to sign anything over $75,000 annually, which is probably where this lands given our size.

AE: You mentioned Notion teams already popping up organically. What's the usage pattern look like?

Sarah: We have maybe 80 people on free or Plus accounts. Product and engineering started it. They love it for specs and documentation. But it's not connected to anything - no company admin, no standardized templates, no AI features. It's kind of orphaned.

AE: Makes sense. I want to show you what a company like yours looks like when that's unified. Can I walk through a quick overview of how Meridian might use Notion as a single operating layer?

Sarah: Yes, please. That's exactly what I want to see.

AE: So the core idea is you keep Jira for engineering ticketing if you want - Notion isn't trying to replace that - but everything around it, the specs, the meeting notes, the OKRs, the cross-functional project tracking, all of that lives in Notion with full AI search and summarization on top of it.

Sarah: What does the AI actually do? Like concretely.

AE: So Notion AI can draft documents from scratch, summarize long pages, translate between languages, but the most powerful thing for Ops is the ability to search across your entire workspace conversationally. You can ask 'what decisions did we make about the enterprise pricing strategy in Q1' and it will surface the relevant docs, meeting notes, and decisions.

Sarah: That's... actually the thing I want most. Okay. What does implementation look like? We've been burned by big rollouts before - we did a Salesforce implementation two years ago that was a disaster.

AE: Totally understand. The good news is Notion onboarding is usually measured in weeks, not months. We'd typically start with a pilot team, get them running on a standardized workspace template, then roll out company-wide with IT and SSO configured. You already have 80 people who are somewhat familiar.

Sarah: What's the SSO story? We're on Okta.

AE: Full SAML SSO, Okta is one of our most common integrations. That's a one-day configuration.

Sarah: Okay. I think the next step is probably getting our Head of Engineering and Head of Product on a demo call. Would you be able to do a deeper technical walkthrough for them?

AE: Absolutely. What does the week of the 20th look like?

Sarah: Send me an invite for the 22nd at 2pm, I'll get them on it.

AE: Done. I'll also send over some case studies from companies at your stage - there's a Ramp case study that I think will resonate a lot.

Sarah: Perfect. Looking forward to it.

label: Executive Briefing - Cascade Ventures
transcript:
AE (Marcus): Ivan, Priya, thanks for blocking this time. I know you're both running at a hundred miles an hour given the Series D close.

Ivan (CTO, Cascade Ventures): We appreciate you coming to us. Honestly, after the funding news went out we've had every vendor in our inbox. You made the shortlist because we're already a heavy Notion shop.

Priya (CFO, Cascade Ventures): And because we need to make some real decisions about our AI stack. We're not going to just buy every AI tool that shows up.

AE: That's exactly the right framing. Tell me about where Notion sits today and what the AI decision looks like.

Ivan: So we have about 1,100 people, and Notion is genuinely the operating system for engineering and product. Every spec, every RFC, every project is in Notion. We have a Business plan today. The question for us is whether we go deeper with Notion AI and Notion Agents, or whether we go with Microsoft Copilot which our sales team is pushing for.

Priya: The CFO angle for me is consolidation. We're paying for Notion, we're paying for Confluence we haven't fully migrated off, we're paying for Asana which the project management office uses, and now I've got the sales team asking for Copilot. I want to go from four tools to one if we can.

AE: What does the Copilot evaluation look like? Is that serious or more of a negotiating posture?

Ivan: It's serious. Our Head of Sales loves it because it integrates with Teams and Outlook. But honestly, Cascade doesn't run on Microsoft infrastructure. We're AWS-native, our team chat is Slack, and asking 1,100 engineers to live in Teams is a non-starter.

AE: That's a real distinction. Let me show you what Notion Agents looks like for an engineering org at your scale, because I think it changes the conversation.

Ivan: Go for it.

AE: So the use case that's generating the most excitement right now is what we're calling the engineering onboarding agent. When a new engineer joins, instead of spending their first two weeks asking questions in Slack, they have a Notion Agent that knows your entire codebase documentation, your architecture decisions, your team norms. They can ask it anything. Okta saw their time-to-first-PR drop by 30% in the pilot.

Ivan: That's genuinely interesting. We're hiring 200 engineers in the next 18 months. Onboarding is already a bottleneck.

Priya: What does enterprise pricing look like? We're at 1,100 seats but growing fast.

AE: For an organization your size with Agents, we're typically in the $25–35 per seat per month range depending on configuration, with volume discounts kicking in at your scale. For 1,100 seats that's roughly $300–400K annually. I know that sounds significant, but if you're collapsing Confluence, Asana, and avoiding a Copilot license, the consolidation math often works in your favor.

Priya: Send me the total cost of ownership model. I want to run the numbers myself.

Ivan: Can we do a technical deep-dive with my Head of Platform Engineering? I want him to see the API and the agent framework before I make any commitments.

AE: Absolutely. I'd suggest pairing him with our Solutions Engineering team. We can do a half-day workshop if that's useful - hands-on with the Notion Agents SDK, not just a demo.

Ivan: That's exactly what we need. Let's set that up.

Priya: And I want the security compliance docs. SOC 2, data residency, all of it. We have new compliance obligations from the Series D.

AE: All of that is available. We're SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, and I can loop in our enterprise security team for a dedicated review call if you want to go deep.

Priya: Yes. Let's do that in parallel.

AE: This is great. So next steps: TCO model from me by end of week, half-day SE workshop with your platform team, and a security review call with Priya's team. Does that capture it?

Ivan: That's it. We want to move fast. If the technical evaluation goes well, we could be signing by end of quarter.

label: Technical Deep-Dive - Ironclad Systems
transcript:
SE (Alex): Thanks for joining today. I'm going to walk you through the technical architecture - SSO, permissions model, API, and our enterprise security controls.

Dave (IT Security Lead, Ironclad Systems): Sure. I'll be honest with you - I have a lot of concerns and I haven't had them answered in the previous calls.

SE: Let's get into it. What are the top ones?

Dave: SSO. We're on a highly customized Okta instance. We have attribute-based access control, we have custom groups that don't map cleanly to standard SCIM provisioning. I tried to set up the Notion SAML integration with a test instance last week and it broke three times.

SE: What was the error you were seeing?

Dave: The attribute mapping for department and role wasn't populating in the user provisioning. So new users were being created but with no workspace access because the groups weren't syncing.

SE: That's usually a configuration issue on the Okta side rather than the Notion side. The SCIM provisioning requires specific attribute names that sometimes don't match your Okta schema. I can connect you with our enterprise support team to go through that setup.

Dave: We've been waiting three weeks for a response from enterprise support.

SE: I apologize for that. I'll follow up directly after this call.

Dave: My second concern is data residency. We have customers in the EU and we have GDPR obligations. Where does Notion store data?

SE: We offer US and EU data residency at the Enterprise plan level. If you're on Enterprise, your workspace can be pinned to EU infrastructure.

Dave: We're not on Enterprise. We're on Business.

SE: Data residency is an Enterprise feature, yes.

Dave: So if we need EU data residency, we have to upgrade.

SE: That's correct. The Business plan stores data in the US.

Dave: That's a problem. We can't move forward without EU data residency. We'd have to upgrade and that's a budget conversation I'm not authorized to have.

SE: I understand. Who would be the right person to have that conversation with?

Dave: I don't know. Honestly, the business champion who was originally driving this left the company two months ago. I'm not sure who's picking this up internally.

SE: Is there anyone else at Ironclad who's actively advocating for Notion?

Dave: Not that I know of. I got looped in because of the security review. I'm not the buyer.

SE: I see. Let me step back - is there an active procurement process here, or has this stalled?

Dave: I honestly don't know. I'm just doing my job reviewing the technical requirements. If the security doesn't pass, it doesn't matter who the champion is.

SE: Fair enough. Let me walk through our security certifications and then the API if that's useful.

Dave: I guess. But I want to flag that we also looked at Confluence last month and they have an on-premises deployment option. We're not ruled out on on-prem. Does Notion have an on-prem offering?

SE: Notion doesn't have an on-premises deployment. It's a cloud-native product.

Dave: That might be a dealbreaker. I'll have to document that.

SE: I appreciate you being direct. Let me make sure I understand the blockers: data residency on Business plan, SSO configuration issues, possible on-prem requirement, and no internal champion since your previous contact left.

Dave: That's accurate.

SE: Is there anything we could address in the next 30 days that would unblock this?

Dave: The SSO issue needs to be fixed regardless. And we need to find out if anyone internally wants to own the budget conversation. I can't answer that.

SE: I'll send you direct resources for the SSO configuration and loop in our enterprise support lead. And I'll follow up with your AE to see if there's a path to finding the right internal stakeholder.
